title: Benchmarking Floquet Master Equations for Periodically Driven Open Quantum Systems
authors: Konrad Mickiewicz, Valentin Link, Walter T. Strunz
abstract: The dynamics of open quantum systems is commonly described by quantum master equations derived under the assumption of weak system-bath coupling and a separation of timescales between system and bath. When the system is additionally subjected to a periodic driving, the validity of the resulting Floquet master equations is further restricted to regimes of weak or high-frequency driving. Here, we benchmark a set of commonly used Floquet master equations for a model of two locally driven spins coupled to a shared Ohmic reservoir at finite temperature. We systematically probe the accuracy of the equations as a function of the driving parameters, thus identifying limits of their applicability. Dynamical maps predicted by each master equation are compared against numerically exact non-Markovian simulations, tracking the full relaxation dynamics. We find that the accuracy of each master equation closely reflects the assumptions underlying its derivation. For the Floquet-Lindblad equation, errors can be strongly amplified near resonances where the secular approximation breaks down, while approaches that avoid the secular approximation perform better and exhibit a more systematic dependence of the error on driving frequency and amplitude.
summary: This paper rigorously benchmarks several analytical master equations designed for periodically driven open quantum systems. By comparing their predictions against numerically exact simulations using a two-spin model, the authors map out the precise parameter regimes where each equation remains accurate. The findings guide practitioners toward selecting robust methods, particularly favoring time-independent Floquet-Redfield approaches over those relying on approximations like the secular limit.
main_problem: The core problem is benchmarking various Floquet master equations used to describe open quantum systems subjected to periodic driving, aiming to pinpoint the limits of their theoretical applicability.
main_result: The accuracy of any given master equation strongly reflects the underlying physical assumptions; specifically, errors are amplified near resonances where approximations like the secular approximation break down.
method: The authors compare predictions from multiple analytical master equations (e.g., Floquet-Lindblad, Redfield) against numerically exact non-Markovian simulations using advanced techniques like uniTEMPO.
model_or_system: The study uses a model of two locally driven spins coupled to a shared Ohmic reservoir at finite temperature, governed by a time-periodic Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Accuracy of the predicted dynamics (e.g., $\langle\sigma_z \otimes\sigma_zangle$ evolution) compared against exact simulations; error measure $\varepsilon(t)$.
important_parameters: Driving frequency ($\omega_d$), driving amplitude ($\epsilon_d$), and temperature/bath spectral density parameters.
main_assumptions: The primary assumptions tested are weak system-bath coupling, the validity of the secular approximation, and the limitations imposed by perturbative expansions (like Magnus series).
figures_summary: Figures compare the error measure ($\varepsilon$) across different master equations as a function of driving frequency and amplitude, generally showing that time-independent Floquet-Redfield performs best.
paper_structure: The paper systematically reviews the derivation of various master equation formalisms (Magnus, Redfield, Lindblad) applicable to periodically driven systems, followed by rigorous benchmarking against exact non-Markovian simulations across varying parameter regimes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a crucial diagnostic tool for researchers developing models for open quantum dynamics under time-periodic driving, helping them select the most reliable theoretical framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Convergence of the dynamical map for the driven two- spin boson mode obtained with uniTEMPO with respect to the bond dimension (left panel) and Trotter time step (right panel). As a reference we used high-accuracy calculations with large bond dimension and small Trotter step. Model parameters are ϵd = 5Ω, ωd = 10Ω, ωA = ωB = Ω, α = 2.5 × 10−4, ωc = 20Ω, β = 0.1/Ω.

FIG. 2. Expectation value of σz ⊗σz for an example dynamics of the driven two-spin boson model with initial state ρ(0) = |11⟩⟨11|. Top row: fast and weak driving regime (Ω/ωd = 0.1, ϵd = 0.1Ω) where our error measure is small for all master equations ε < 0.05. Bottom row: slow and strong driving regime (Ω/ωd = 1.3, ϵd = 4Ω) where our error measure of the master equations is large ε > 0.2, with an exception of the time-independent Floquet-Redfield for which ε < 0.05. Remaining simulation parameters: ωA = ωB = Ω, α = 2.5 × 10−4, ωc = 20Ω, β = 0.1/Ω.

FIG. 3. Error measure (53) for the Magnus-Redfield and Magnus-Lindblad master equations as a function of the driv- ing frequency ωd and the driving amplitude ϵd for resonant (ωA = ωB = Ω, top row) and detuned (ωA = 2ωB = 2Ω, bottom row) spins. The validity of the master equations is restricted to the high-frequency and low-amplitude regime (bottom left corner of each plot) as a result of the underlying first-order Magnus expansion. Remaining simulation param- eters: α = 2.5 × 10−4, ωc = 20Ω, β = 0.1/Ω.

FIG. 4. Error measure (53) for the weakly driven Lindblad, time-independent Floquet-Redfield and Floquet-Lindblad master equations as a function of the driving frequency ωd and the driving amplitude ϵd for the resonant (ωA = ωB = Ω, top row) and detuned (ωA = 2ωB = 2Ω, bottom row) spins. The validity differs for each master equation and each regime. We achieve the overall best accuracy for the time-independent Floquet-Redfield equation. Remaining simulation parame- ters: α = 2.5 × 10−4, ωc = 20Ω, β = 0.1/Ω.

FIG. 6. Error measure (53) for the Magnus-Redfield and Magnus-Lindblad master equations as a function of the driv- ing frequency ωd and the driving amplitude ϵd, as in Fig. 3 but for lower bath temperature β = 1/Ωand α = 1.25×10−3.
